item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, it admittedly pulled me out of the reality. Still a good skip generally imo.

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Image Sources

https://www.flickr.com/photos/westmidlandspolice/6634887781/

CC BY-SA 2.0

https://www.flickr.com/photos/james_nash/4758670232/

CC BY-SA 2.0

https://www.flickr.com/photos/lorenkerns/14607567223

CC BY 2.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found an AI that generates inspirational nonsense. It reminds me of this article, so here's a link. You know, for fun. I figured it would be handy to have a program that could generate this kind of humor whenever we want.

Example Output
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is definitely my home town.
Also the slogans are hilarious, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last picture's gun should be backwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing about this SCP that bugs me far more than it ought to: judging by the "National Theatre" logo on those banners, the image for the second poster was taken in London, England. So what's it doing in an anomalous ad campaign in Illinois?

item.title: Re: Nice crit there, pal.
item.content:encoded:
I probably could have not been sarcastic, but the repeated low-effort posts, combined with their vehemency is quite frustrating to witness, especially given the fact that they've been spoken to in a formal and official capacity. In a vacuum, I wouldn't have looked twice at that post, but taken altogether it paints an ugly picture.

This is more 'getting rocks off by low-key trolling' than it is doing the slightest thing for any other user - critique does not exist for one to throw out quips like you're some kind of genius.

Simple, brief, opinionated posts are perfectly acceptable. Having that be all one posts, complete with using 'Downvoted' like a punchline, is behavior I do not wish to see people replicate. Apologies.

item.title: Re: Nice crit there, pal.
item.content:encoded: I guess the problem herein lies with the user's continued low-effort shitcrit on articles, but this is a thing that could have been handled without sarcasm yes. (Also, I'm no longer staff.)

item.title: Re: Nice crit there, pal.
item.content:encoded:
Is this an in-joke that I'm missing? Otherwise the sarcasm comes across as unnecessarily vehement, shaggy - especially coming from staff and when it's not aimed at any of the similar comments above from other staff (Deci, moose, Voct).

It's fine to say "I don't like this" or "I don't think this is good" on a posted work. Not necessarily the most helpful thing to say (in the same way that saying "I like this" or "I think this is good" isn't particularly helpful), but it's fine.

item.title: Nice crit there, pal.
item.content:encoded:
Boy, if everyone could leave such vague and meaningless "critique", we might all be able to bring our collective writing up to snuff.

You should write a guide on how exactly you do what it is you do. It's so goddamn insightful and really very enlightening.

Such a shame!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't do enough with the wonderful material you have. Such a shame!

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels incomplete.

Somebody's pulling a series of anomalous advertising campaigns, OK, that's a good start. But…so? We don't know who is behind it, we don't know why they're doing it, there seems to be no progression toward a goal, it's just happening. The effects are an ordinary compulsion to do some strange stuff, that's not even unusual.

I don't see a hook to pull my interest, and it's not a -J so my chuckles aren't enough of a reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thats a good point. Luckily for me I almost never know the title of a scip unless I specifically search for it.

Ive always found something eerie about stock images and their soulless-ness so the images really worked for me. The bizarre grammar was the icing on top.

What is an advertising folder by the way? It seems to be technical term rather than blanket.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP, but I feel the impact of the last image could be improved if the SCP wasn't named after it. The sinister turn is still strong, but perhaps not as effective ss it could be due to being pretty much laid out from the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't anomalous. I mean, who can say no to animalesque cross-dressers? {*drools*}

But seriously, this is a well-written skip; started off humorous and keeping it at a constant rate while building up tension to reveal why it deserves keter class. All without complicated background history and long interviews. Loved this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely falls into the category of SCPs I call…

"Um… What?"

It's very bizarre and surreal in a really good way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…good grief.

Sorry for the Necropost, but I think you just explained the Daily Mail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surreal, and slightly ominous turn with the bullet resistance part. I think that deserves an up vote in itself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So that's why they are called "shots".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP. It's kinda like "Simon says" in a weird propaganda-ish, poster, media, type of way. +1

NOT EDIT: I showed my friend at school the last picture and he thought it was funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose I'm "that idiot": Was "meh" until I saw the last image. Genuine laughter == upvote. Thanks. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, this seems like is should be a -J instead. It's written formaly, but the posters are just too funny

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat idea, with lots of potential, but not enough execution. Not voting yet.

I'm pleased that the Foundation took the time to check on the survival rate of the Golden Lion Tamarin, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, I feel like this doesn't go quite far enough. It's like, anomalous materials that don't make sense and make you do weird, potentially fatal shit: here are some examples.

On the other hand, that just means there's room to expand on the weird shit. And I just love skips that are basically collections of nonsense. Probably the best part of this is the question mark at the end of the cardboard boxes one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CDC-SCP-4298, The Foundation Guidebook For Anomalous Pathological Conditions, Edition 3, Page 782

Duuude, you missed the perfect opportunity for a tongue-in-cheek DSM-SCP reference. ;)

As for the article itself, I had to downvote because there's so very much awkward phrasing. It kept tripping me up, and badly. The visuals are good, but the concept's a bit bland, and the wording made it difficult to get through a paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy as hell… Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's rather dull. I love the last poster, but the rest of it… eh. Including the explanation of the poster. It's a great idea — and a wasted idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this a lot. It's short and to the point. Comical, in a way, but also sinister.

Also, that last poster is one of my favorite images on the site now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The weird text and bizarre language juxtaposed with the banal imagery is a neat device, however it's one that I think was carried off better in SCP-2557. In this particular case, what the article is at heart is a garden variety compulsion effect.

As with almost all articles that rely on a compulsion effect, I can't help but feel like the reader is "on rails," so to speak. I am not drawn to any conclusions about what a phenomenon means because the writer carefully considered the salient qualities of the object and what kind of reactions they might engender. Instead I am shown that this thing will affect everyone that comes across it immediately and in the exact same way, and the dynamic becomes something weaker. It's not "this thing is unsettling/dangerous/scary because it is X," but rather it's "this thing is unsettling/dangerous/scary because I the author have directly declared that it is unsettling/dangerous/scary."

This is a general, long-running issue of mine, so it's not a feature unique to your writing or anything. But I was definitely hoping for some kind of different direction from the initial prompt than the one you took.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I like this a lot. It's a bit funny, a bit weird, and a bit tragic all at the same time. Although, I was slightly disappointed to see the second incident stop short after the third poster. Was hoping to see a little addendum there. Oh well. Still good overall. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in the chat, this is a solid scip. Maybe it would be better if the sinister angle were played up, maybe it would be better with logs, but that dosn't matter. What matters is that, as it is, this scip works fine. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Prior made quite a cool suggestion in chat about a collaboration page for this thing. If it's succesful enough, i might create it and it can bring some immersion to the article. I did not want to drone on and on on weird things to make people laugh a lot, because i was just going for plain funny-ish WTF. I appreciate the feedback, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like it can go so big and go so many places for potential breaches and stories and how the Foundation could go to contain this but it just feels a bit fizzled out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That rifle? That's a Stoeger X10 or X20. I have one. It does not increase bullet resistance as much as you'd think.

Neat skip. Solid combination of WTF, comedy, and tragedy.
